title: practical english usage - Part 101
content:
I don't see much  point in arguing about it.

There's not much  difference between 'begin' and 'start'.

difficulty, chance and question.

other determiners. Common examples are difference, point, reason, idea, change, Some countable abstract nouns can be used uncountably after little, much  and 2 not    much      difference If you buy one of these, you get plenty of car for your money.

I've got too much  nose and not enough chin.

There's enough paint for 20 square metres of wall.

enough, plenty of or ɑ  lot of) in order to express the idea of amount.

Singular countable nouns are sometimes used as uncountables (e.g. with much, 1  20 square        metres        of   wall advanced       points          __ 120 countable       and   uncountable         nouns: For more information on the use of articles with countable and uncountable nouns, ►    137.

For the with measles, flu, etc, ►     142.15.

I've got toothache. (BrE)      I have a toothache. (AmE) generally countable if they refer to particular attacks of pain. Compare: usually uncountable in British English. In American English, these words are a headache. However, toothache, earache, stomach ache and backache are The  words for some minor ailments are countable: e.g. a cold, a sore throat, There is a lot of flu around at the moment.

If you've already had measles, you can't get it again.

those ending in -s.

The  names of illnesses are usually singular uncountable in English, including 7 illnesses For more about time, ►    608; for life, ►    513.

Megan and I had a very interesting conversation.

- I need to practise conversation.

I had some frightening thoughts in the night.

- Your plan needs more thought.

It's a pity it's raining.

- It's hard to feel pity for people like that.

I had some strange experiences last week.

- She hasn 't got enough experience for the job.

He's had a really difficult life.

- Life is complicated.

grammar •  120   countable and uncountable nouns: advanced points For details, ►     117.7.

Many thanks for your help, (          Much  thank. . .) koi (Bin      Fhe-Uovef custom has-...) The Dover customs have found a large shipment of cocaine.

but not                               or I've bought the groceries, (          .. . a grocery,  ... three groceries/) meaning, and cannot normally be used with numbers.

Some uncountable nouns are plural. They  have no singular forms with the same 5 plural     uncountables:         the    groceries;         the    customs damage. ..) not His speech did serious damage to his chances of election. (     .. .-a-serieus not It's interesting work. (     .. .an interesting work.) not He speaks excellent English. (     . .. an excellent English.) not We're having terrible weather. (     .. .a terrible weather.) not My father enjoys very good health. (     ...a very good health.) adjective.

uncountable nouns cannot be used with a/an at all, even when they have an Note that these nouns cannot normally be used in the plural, and that most I need a good sleep.

You've been a great help.

(NOT   ...to have good education.) My parents wanted me to have a good education.

That child shows a surprising understanding of adult behaviour.

She has always had a deep distrust of strangers.

(N     ...with first-class knowledge of German.) We need a secretary with a first-class knowledge of German.

meaning in some way.

emotions and mental activity - we  often use ɑ/ɑn  when we  are limiting their With certain uncountable nouns - especially nouns referring to human 4 a/an    with    uncountable         nouns:    a  first-class         knowledge Gulliver's Travels (novel by Jonathan Swift) Did you meet anybody exciting on your travels?

He goes running in all weathers.

A  few uncountable nouns have plural uses in fixed expressions.

3 in   all   weathers;         on   your      travels I have difficulty (in) remembering faces, (NOT I have difficulties ...) Note the expression have difficulty (in) .. .ing.

There's some question of our getting a new Managing Director.

Do you think we have much  chance of catching the train?
